title: Multiplication
shortDescription: A parent's guide to multiplication: building real understanding before the times table, memory tricks that actually stick, two-digit multiplication, and the slip-ups almost every child makes.
importance: Multiplication is not just one more item on a list, it is the floor that almost everything else stands on. Division, fractions, percentages, area, factoring, all of them lean on a child knowing that 7 times 8 is 56 without stopping to count. A child who owns the times table feels calm in math class, while one who hesitates struggles with each new topic. It shows up in daily life too: the cost of six packs of juice, how many days are in eight weeks, how many seats fill four rows of chairs.
howToTeach: Start with the meaning, not the memorizing: explain that 3 x 4 is three groups of four, which is the same as 4 plus 4 plus 4.
Teach the table in order of difficulty, beginning with the easy 2s, 5s, and 10s, then the 3s and 4s, and saving the trickier 6s, 7s, and 8s for last.
Use a grid of rows and columns so the child can see that 3 x 4 is literally a rectangle of dots, which makes the answer feel concrete.
Show shortcuts that lighten the load, like the finger trick for the 9s and the pattern that every multiple of 5 ends in 0 or 5.
Practice the facts mixed up rather than always in sequence, because a child who only knows the table in order does not truly know it yet.

examples.explanation: Four sevens means 7 plus 7 plus 7 plus 7, which adds up to 28. Once it is solid, the child should recall it straight from the table without adding.
Use the finger trick: hold up ten fingers and fold down the sixth one. The 5 fingers left of the gap are the tens and the 4 to the right are the units, giving 54.
If this one is forgotten, break it apart: 7 x 4 is 28, and 7 x 4 again is another 28, so 28 plus 28 equals 56.
Start with the units: 3 x 4 is 12, so write the 2 and carry the 1. Then the tens: 2 x 4 is 8, plus the carried 1 makes 9. The answer is 92.

commonMistakes: Mixing up similar facts such as 6 x 7 and 7 x 8 when the memorizing was mechanical and never tied to any meaning.
Forgetting to carry in two-digit multiplication, so 23 x 4 comes out as 82 instead of the correct 92.
Swapping multiplication for addition and thinking 3 x 4 is 7, which is worth fixing by returning to the idea of repeated groups.
Knowing the table only in order and freezing the moment a single fact is asked out of sequence.
parentTips: Practice for five minutes every day, because short daily reps beat one long hour once a week by a wide margin.
Turn it into a song or a quick family quiz at the dinner table so it feels like a game rather than a chore.
Point out the patterns, for example that every multiple of 5 ends in 0 or 5, which removes a whole column of guessing.
Never force mechanical drilling before the child understands what multiplication means, since understanding always comes first.
When your child gets stuck on one fact, split it into smaller facts they already know and rebuild it from there.
